title: W7 B Partial Derivatives
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW–Sydney

Week 7B — Term 1, 2026

1/15  Partial Derivatives

2/15   Examples

Example 1

Let S = Rn ++ and f : S → R be defined by

f (x) = xα 1 α2 αn 1 x2 · · · xn .

The derivative of f at x ∈ S is the linear map Df (x) represented by the gradient vector ∇f (x) ∈ Rn , where   ∇f (x) = α 1 x1 f (x), α x2 f (x), , α xn n f (x) .

It would be hard to verify that this is the derivative of a Cobb-Douglas function using definitions.

3/15   Partial Derivatives

Instead of computing Df (x) from definition, suppose we distort xi while keeping x−i fixed. This leads to the notion of partial derivatives.

Definition 1

Let S ⊆ Rn be open and f : S → R. The i-th partial derivative of f at x is defined by f (x1 , , xi−1 , xi + t, xi+1 , , xn ) − f (x1 , , xn ) lim , t→0 t

provided this limit exists.

4/15   Partial Derivatives

Instead of computing Df (x) from definition, suppose we distort xi while keeping x−i fixed. This leads to the notion of partial derivatives.

Definition 1

Let S ⊆ Rn be open and f : S → R. The i-th partial derivative of f at x is defined by f (x1 , , xi−1 , xi + t, xi+1 , , xn ) − f (x1 , , xn ) lim , t→0 t

provided this limit exists.

Write (x1 , , xi−1 , xi + t, xi+1 , , xn ) as x + t ei . The i-th partial derivative of f at x is then ∂ f (x) f (x + t ei ) − f (x) = lim . ∂ xi t→0 t

4/15   Example

Example 2

Let f : R2 → R be defined by f (x1 , x2 ) = x21 x2 + x2 .

Partial derivative with respect to x1 : fix x2 and compute ∂f (x) f (x1 + t, x2 ) − f (x1 , x2 ) ∂x1 t→0 t [(x1 + t)2 x2 + x2 ] − [x21 x2 + x2 ] t→0 t [(x1 + t)2 − x21 ] x2 t→0 t (2x1 t + t2 ) x2 t→0 t h i = lim 2x1 x2 + t x2 = 2x1 x2 . t→0

5/15   Partial Derivatives vs the Derivative

The relationship between partial derivatives and the derivative Df is more complicated than what appears to be.

Intuitively, the partial derivative restricts the way we approach x. ◦ Instead of u → x, we move only in the i-th dimension.

6/15   Partial Derivatives vs the Derivative

The relationship between partial derivatives and the derivative Df is more complicated than what appears to be.

Intuitively, the partial derivative restricts the way we approach x. ◦ Instead of u → x, we move only in the i-th dimension.

One can show that if f : S → R is differentiable at x ∈ U , then the i-th partial derivative must exist, for i = 1, , n. Moreover,   ∂ f (x) ∂ f (x) Df (x)⊤ = ∇f (x) = , ... , ∈ Rn . ∂ x1 ∂ xn

But the converse is not true!

Knowing that each i-th partial derivative exists at x is not enough to assert the existence of the linear approximation Df (x).

6/15   Partial Derivatives vs the Derivative

Example 3

Let f (x1 , x2 ) be defined by   x21 x22 , (x1 , x2 ) ̸= (0, 0) x1 + x2 f (x1 , x2 ) = 0, (x1 , x2 ) = (0, 0).

One can show that both partial derivatives exist at (0, 0) but f is not differentiable at (0, 0).

◦ Intuitively, if x2 = 0 then f (x1 , 0) is constant.

◦ But f is not continuous at (0, 0), hence it cannot admit a derivative.

7/15   Existence of Derivative

Proposition 1

Let S ⊆ Rn be open and f : S → R. If all partial derivatives of f exist in an open neighbourhood of x ∈ S and are continuous at x, then f is differentiable at x. Moreover, ∂ f (x) Df (x) ei = , for all i = 1, , n. ∂ xi

8/15   Existence of Derivative

Proposition 1

Let S ⊆ Rn be open and f : S → R. If all partial derivatives of f exist in an open neighbourhood of x ∈ S and are continuous at x, then f is differentiable at x. Moreover, ∂ f (x) Df (x) ei = , for all i = 1, , n. ∂ xi

Exercise 1

For f (x) = xα αn 1 · · · xn , show that at x̂ > 0

∂ f (x̂) αi = f (x̂). ∂ xi x̂i

Conclude that f is differentiable everywhere on Rn ++ .

8/15  Directional Derivatives

9/15   Directional Derivatives

The j-th partial derivative of f at x̂ specifies approaching x̂ ∈ Rn in the direction ej = (0, , 0, 1, 0, , 0). ◦ This is why we write x̂ + t ej for t ∈ R.

Consider any directional vector u such that ∥u∥ = 1.

10/15   Directional Derivatives

The j-th partial derivative of f at x̂ specifies approaching x̂ ∈ Rn in the direction ej = (0, , 0, 1, 0, , 0). ◦ This is why we write x̂ + t ej for t ∈ R.

Consider any directional vector u such that ∥u∥ = 1. ◦ Approaching x̂ in this direction obtains

f (x̂ + t u) − f (x̂) lim . t→0 t

◦ When exists, this limit is called the directional derivative of f at x̂ in the direction u.

10/15   Directional Derivatives vs the Derivative

Proposition 2

Let f be a real-valued function defined on the open set U ⊆ Rn . If f is differentiable at x̂ ∈ U and u is any directional vector in Rn , then the directional derivative of f at x̂ in the direction u exists and equals Df (x̂) u.

Proof Omitted

11/15   Directional Derivatives vs the Derivative

Proposition 2

Let f be a real-valued function defined on the open set U ⊆ Rn . If f is differentiable at x̂ ∈ U and u is any directional vector in Rn , then the directional derivative of f at x̂ in the direction u exists and equals Df (x̂) u.

Proof Omitted

Note of course that for all j = 1, , n, ∂ f (x̂) = Df (x̂) ej . ∂ xj

11/15   Example

Example 4

Let f : R2 → R be defined by f (x1 , x2 ) = x21 x2 + x2 .

Partial derivatives:

∂f (x) ∂f (x) = 2x1 x2 and = x21 + 1. ∂x1 ∂x2

12/15   Example

Example 4

Let f : R2 → R be defined by f (x1 , x2 ) = x21 x2 + x2 .

Partial derivatives:

∂f (x) ∂f (x) = 2x1 x2 and = x21 + 1. ∂x1 ∂x2

For any u = (u1 , u2 ), the directional derivative is ⊤ Df (x) u = 2x1 x2 , x21 + 1 (u1 , u2 )

= 2x1 x2 u1 + (x21 + 1)u2 .

12/15  Higher Order Derivatives

13/15   Second Order Derivatives

When f : S → R is differentiable, for every x ∈ S we can find and associated ∇f (x) = Df (x)⊤ in Rn .

We can think of Df : S → Rn as a function in its own right. ◦ If Df is continuous everywhere, we say that f is continuously differentiable.

◦ We can ask if Df is differentiable at x̂.

◦ When Df is differentiable at x̂, its derivative D [ Df (x̂) ] is

 ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂)  ∂x1 ∂x1 ∂x1 ∂x2 ··· ∂x1 ∂xn    2 ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂)    ∂ f (x̂) ···  ∂x2 ∂x1 ∂x2 ∂x2 ∂x2 ∂xn  D2 f (x̂) = D [ Df (x̂) ] =   .   . .. .. ..   .     2 2 ∂ f (x̂) ∂ f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂xn ∂x1 ∂xn ∂x2 ··· ∂xn ∂xn

14/15   Higher Order Derivatives

The matrix D2 f (x̂) is called the Hessian matrix of f at x̂:  ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂)  ∂x1 ∂x1 ∂x1 ∂x2 ··· ∂x1 ∂xn    2 ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂)    ∂ f (x̂) ···  ∂x2 ∂x1 ∂x2 ∂x2 ∂x2 ∂xn  D f (x̂) =   .   . .. .. ..   .     ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂xn ∂x1 ∂xn ∂x2 ··· ∂xn ∂xn

When the function f is smooth (i.e., Df (x) is itself continuously differentiable), one can show that this matrix is symmetric:

∂ 2 f (x̂) ∂ 2 f (x̂) = , for all i, j = 1, , n. ∂xi ∂xj ∂xj ∂xi
